rationale: The citing paper discusses Anderson et al. (2010) in-text as concrete prior evidence that tax-related nexus changes affect Internet sales, not just as part of a generic citation list. It then uses that prior result to justify its own tax-based logic in hypothesis development (H2) and in measurement, treating local offline sales tax variation as a proxy for relative offline versus online price advantage. This is a meaningful conceptual and empirical input to the citing paper's model setup, even though the focal contribution is preference isolation rather than taxation itself.
relation_description: The citing paper applies Anderson et al. (2010)'s sales-tax and online-search insight to its own context by using local sales taxes to model geographic variation in shopping costs and by building a hypothesis about how tax-based price effects interact with preference isolation.
